Title: Foundations: how LLMs and tokens actually work
Why: Everything else — cost, context limits, prompting — makes sense once you understand tokens and how a model predicts text.

Steps.Text: Read a plain-English intro to how LLMs predict the next token.
Play with a tokenizer to see how text becomes tokens.
Understand context windows and why long chats cost more.

Title: Prompting well
Why: A clear prompt is the cheapest, fastest quality win you have. Most 'the AI is dumb' moments are prompt problems.

Steps.Text: Learn role/task framing, step-by-step instructions and output formatting.
Practice few-shot examples to lock in a format.
Try terse 'caveman' prompts and compare quality vs token cost.

Title: Context engineering & token optimization
Why: As soon as you build something real, cost and context limits bite. Knowing what to put in (and leave out) is the core skill.

Steps.Text: Learn prompt caching to cut cost on repeated large contexts.
Practice trimming/summarizing history in long sessions.
Route cheap tasks to small models, hard tasks to frontier models.

Title: RAG: answer over your own data
Why: The standard way to make a model use your private/up-to-date knowledge without retraining it.

Steps.Text: Understand embeddings and vector similarity search.
Build a minimal RAG pipeline: chunk -> embed -> retrieve -> answer.
Improve retrieval quality with reranking and contextual retrieval.

Title: Tool use & function calling
Why: The mechanism that lets a model do things — search, query a DB, run code — instead of just talking.

Steps.Text: Learn how to define tools and handle tool-call results.
Wire one real tool (e.g. a web search or DB query) end to end.
Handle errors and retries so a bad tool call doesn't break the run.

Title: Building agents & agentic loops
Why: Going from one-shot prompts to a loop that plans, acts and self-corrects is what unlocks real automation.

Steps.Text: Read Anthropic's guide to building effective agents.
Implement a ReAct-style loop: think -> act -> observe -> repeat.
Add a stopping condition and verification so the loop ends well.

Title: MCP: connect your agent to tools
Why: The emerging standard for plugging AI into your systems in a reusable way instead of one-off integrations.

Steps.Text: Read the Model Context Protocol intro.
Connect an existing MCP server (filesystem, GitHub) to a client.
Write a tiny MCP server exposing one of your own tools.

Title: Run local & open-weight models
Why: For privacy, offline use, cheap high-volume calls and experimentation, running your own model is a superpower.

Steps.Text: Install Ollama and run a small model locally.
Learn quantization (e.g. 4-bit GGUF) to fit your hardware.
Point a tool (Cline, a script) at your local model via its API.

Title: Evals: measure quality before you ship
Why: Without a test set you're tuning blind. Evals tell you whether a prompt/model change actually helped.

Steps.Text: Collect 10-20 real examples with expected outcomes.
Score model output automatically (exact match, rules, or an LLM judge).
Re-run evals on every prompt/model change to catch regressions.

Title: Pick the right coding tool & workflow
Why: Using Copilot where you needed an agent (or vice-versa) wastes time and tokens. Match the tool to the task.

Steps.Text: Use an IDE assistant (Copilot) for inline completions while typing.
Use a coding agent (Claude Code, Codex, Cline) for multi-file tasks that need to run tests.
Give agents project memory (a CLAUDE.md / rules file) so they stop re-learning your repo.
